coredata.dc:title: Metal ions-driven multi-enzyme activity of PEI-carbon dots-based nanozyme to improve antibacterial ability

coredata.dc:description: How to effectively eliminate bacterial biofilms still poses significant challenges, and the further improvement of enzymatic catalytic activity is crucial for the removal of bacterial biofilms. Herein, eleven metal ions were engineered onto polyethyleneimine chelated Mn-doped-carbon dots (PEI@Mn-CDs) through post-synthesis modification to further enhance the catalytic activity and endow the new enzyme-like activity. It is worth noting that the obtained PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid by Cu+ addition showed wider pH application range, more surprising multienzyme-like activity and better antibacterial activity. Compared with PEI@Mn-CDs, the V max of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs is increased by 29.7 times, and the minimum inhibitory concentration can reach 4 µg/mL. Mechanism investigation revealed that Cu+ as the additional catalytic sites was integrated with PEI@Mn-CDs by the metal-amine/carboxyl coordination strategy, which is conducive to the improvement of electron transfer efficiency and the generation of free radicals. Furthermore, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs could enter the inside of bacteria and produce free radicals to kill bacteria, showing great potential against bacteria, drug-resistant bacteria and biofilms. This work provides an effective strategy for the design of highly efficient antibacterial and antibiofilm nanozymes.

head.ce:title: Metal ions-driven multi-enzyme activity of PEI-carbon dots-based nanozyme to improve antibacterial ability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: How to effectively eliminate bacterial biofilms still poses significant challenges, and the further improvement of enzymatic catalytic activity is crucial for the removal of bacterial biofilms. Herein, eleven metal ions were engineered onto polyethyleneimine chelated Mn-doped-carbon dots (PEI@Mn-CDs) through post-synthesis modification to further enhance the catalytic activity and endow the new enzyme-like activity. It is worth noting that the obtained PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid by Cu+ addition showed wider pH application range, more surprising multienzyme-like activity and better antibacterial activity. Compared with PEI@Mn-CDs, the V max of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs is increased by 29.7 times, and the minimum inhibitory concentration can reach 4 µg/mL. Mechanism investigation revealed that Cu+ as the additional catalytic sites was integrated with PEI@Mn-CDs by the metal-amine/carboxyl coordination strategy, which is conducive to the improvement of electron transfer efficiency and the generation of free radicals. Furthermore, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs could enter the inside of bacteria and produce free radicals to kill bacteria, showing great potential against bacteria, drug-resistant bacteria and biofilms. This work provides an effective strategy for the design of highly efficient antibacterial and antibiofilm nanozymes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections pose a serious threat to human health and healthcare, both in terms of incidence and cost [1,2]. Due to antibiotic resistance, microbial contamination caused by resistant bacteria and their biofilms has been considered one of the most serious threats to human health [3]. Biofilms represent a special bacterial community that builds an efficient physical and chemical barrier by producing extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) to protect embedded bacteria from antibiotics and host immune defense [4]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop innovative strategies to combat multi-resistant bacteria and their biofilms [5].

Due to their low toxicity, low drug resistance, excellent membrane permeability and biocompatibility, artificial nanozymes have demonstrated potential in combating drug-resistant bacteria and eliminating biofilms [4]. Nanozymes are nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-like properties, and possess the ability to generate reactive oxygen species (such as
OH), thereby causing irreversible damage to bacteria, disrupting cell structures to inhibit bacterial growth, and achieving antibacterial activity [6,7]. Unlike antibiotics that exert antibacterial effects through a single target, nanozymes can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and employ multiple attack modes to destroy and kill bacteria including physical disruption and chemical assault, thereby significantly reducing the probability of drug resistance. Nanozymes are typically nanomaterials fabricated from carbon based materials [8,9], metal-based nanomaterials [10], precious metals [11], polymers [12], etc., and exhibit various catalytic activities such as peroxidase, oxidase, and superoxide-like activity [13]. Owing to their small size (1–10 nm), large specific surface area, low toxicity, and good stability, carbon dots (CDs) are highly suitable for substrate binding and show enhanced peroxidase (POD)-like activity [14,15]. CDs as a kind of nanozymes have garnered extensive attention in the antibacterial field and possess broad application prospects, which represent the most promising nanomaterials for replacing natural enzymes [16,17]. Ye et al reported the synthesis of chitosan grafted Fe-doped-carbon dots (CS@Fe/CDs) for bacteria biofilm eradication in food safety and environmental protection [3]. Cui et al prepared chitosan-chelated carbon dots-based nanozyme (CS@Fe-N CDs) with super peroxidase activity and antibacterial ability for wound healing [15,18]. Although significant progress has been made in the antibacterial application of CDs-based nanozymes, the relatively lower catalytic activities (compared with natural enzymes) and the relatively weak binding capacity to bacteria may seriously restrict the application of drug-resistant bacteria and their biofilms [19].

To date, several strategies have been proposed by researchers to enhance the catalytic activity of nanozymes, such as surface modification [20], heteroatom doping [21], morphology control [22], ultrasound and infrared assistance [23,24], etc. Among them, modifying or doping nanozymes with metal ions is a reliable and prevalent strategy to obtain highly active nanozymes. For instance, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ce4+ have been utilized to modify or dope carbon dots-based nanozymes or metal–organic framework (MOF) nanozymes to boost their peroxidase or oxidase-like activities [25–27]. Fe3+ and Cu2+ can also be employed simultaneously for doping to prepare nanomaterials and acquire nanozymes with high peroxidase-like activity through synergy [28,6]. It is notable that few studies have directly interacted metal ions with the fabricated nanozymes to further enhance the catalytic activity of nanozymes. Generally, metal ions such as Cu2+ and Cu+ themselves possess almost no enzyme-like activity [25]. But their application in interaction with nanozymes, the improvement of their catalytic activity is foreseeable, as Cu2+ and Cu+ metal ions can also serve as catalytic centers to participate in enzyme-like catalytic reactions.

In recent years, polyethyleneimine (PEI) has been used as a cationic antibacterial agent due to its amine groups on its molecular structure, which become positively charged in water [29]. Taking advantage of this property, PEI is widely used to promote cell adhesion to the negatively charged membrane surface of biological cells through electrostatic interactions [8]. However, the antibacterial effect is not significant when PEI is used directly as an antibacterial agent or coated on the surface of nanomaterials [30,31]. In addition, PEI can also form chelates with metal ions such as Cu and Mn.

In this study, we propose using PEI and Mn as precursor materials for the preparation of CDs, and then using the amine/carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs to interact with metal ions to design nanozymes, effectively killing resistant bacteria and disrupting their biofilms (Fig. 1 ). Eleven non-toxic metal ions are used for the post-modification of PEI@Mn-CDs to prepare PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs (Xn+ means the metal ions). It is worth noting that the changes of metal ions have significant effects on the enzyme activity and antibacterial activity of PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs. Interestingly, Cu+ and Cu2+ addition could obviously increase the POD-like activity and produce other multi-enzyme activities (i.e. OXD-like, laccase-like and CAT-like activities). The V max of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was 29.7-fold higher than that of PEI@Mn-CDs. The enhancement of catalytic activity and antibacterial activity by Cu+ and Cu2+ addition had also been systematically studied. Furthermore, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) could effectively promote the production of free radicals (i.e.
O2 −,
OH and 1O2), resulting in biofilm eradication of representative bacterial (including E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA). The work broadens the design strategy of nanozyme, and carbon dots-based nanozymes provide a new direction for bacterial biofilm-triggered microbial contaminations, such as wound bacterial infections, water, food, and environment.

PEI@Mn-CDs: 0.2 g of PEI, 0.2 g of citric acid, 0.458 g of manganese acetate, and 0.4 g of o-phenylenediamine were taken and dissolved in 20 mL of pure water. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, the above solution was subsequently transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene microwave digestion tank and reacted at 180 °C for 2 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature and finally filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the PEI@Mn-CDs solution.

PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs: 5 mL of the prepared PEI@Mn-CDs (17.8 mg/mL) solution and 5 mL of CuCl (17.8 mg/mL) solution were taken and stirred for 20 min. After standing overnight, the solution was subjected to centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and this process was repeated twice. The resulting solid was designated as PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid), and the liquid was referred to as PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs. The solid product was washed three times with deionized water, and then dried in a vacuum oven. The solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain the PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (liquid) for further use.

PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs (Xn+ = Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sn4+, Ce2+, Mg2+ and Ca2+): Considering that some metal ions are inherently highly toxic, 10 common metal ions (Xn+ = Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Sn4+, Ce2+, Mg2+ and Ca2+) were selected to substitute for the CuCl employed in the preparation process of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs were fabricated under the same conditions. All synthesized PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs were in the liquid state, with no obvious solids generated. The aforementioned solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane for further use.

TMB is often used as a substrate to evaluate the POD-like activities of nanozymes through color changes during catalysis. In the experiment, 20 µL of PEI@Mn-CDs or PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid/liquid) or PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs (4 mg/mL) were mixed with 100 µL each of TMB solution (5 mM) and H2O2 solution (50 mM) containing NaAc-HAc (pH 4), and then diluted with deionized water to a final volume of 4 mL. After thorough mixing, the reaction was conducted at room temperature for 15 min, and the absorbance of the oxidized TMB (oxTMB) was measured at 654 nm. To conduct an in-depth analysis of the catalytic efficiency of different nanozymes towards TMB and H2O2, 20 µL of the nanozyme (4 mg/mL) was mixed with different concentrations of H2O2 (0.25–2 mM) or TMB (0.025–0.2 mM), respectively, and the absorbance change of the system within 0–200 s at a wavelength of 654 nm was measured. According to our previous study [6], the Michaelis constant (K m) and the maximum reaction rate (V max) of the nanozymes were obtained using the Michaelis-Menten curve and the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot:

where v is the reaction rate, [S] is the concentration of the substrate (H2O2 or TMB), K m is the Michaelis constant, and V max is the maximal reaction rate.

Methylene blue (MB) was employed as a hydroxyl radical probe to identify OH by observing the alteration of MB absorbance at 660 nm catalyzed by PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs + H2O2. Specifically, MB (5 mM, 60 μL) was mixed with H2O2 (50 mM, 100 μL) and PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (4 mg/mL, 100 μL) in acetic acid buffer and reacted at room temperature for 4 h. The variation of absorbance at 660 nm was assessed by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The degree of reduction was positively correlated with the quantity of OH. Simultaneously, the existence of OH, O2 −, and 1O2 was confirmed by electron spin resonance (ESR). PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (4 mg/mL, 100 μL) or PEI@Mn-CDs, H2O2 (50 mM, 100 μL), and DMPO (25 mM) were added to 2 mL NaAc-HAc (pH 4). At the onset of the reaction, 0 min and 10 min were recorded, respectively.

To identify the main active oxygen free radical species, under identical conditions, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), β-carotene, and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperoxyl (TEMPO) were respectively added to solutions containing 20 μL PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (4 mg/mL), 100 μL acetate buffer solution (pH 4), and 100 μL H2O2. After a 10 min reaction, 100 μL TMB (5 mM) was added and diluted to 3 mL, and the reaction was conducted at room temperature for 15 min. The reduction of oxTMB absorption by the scavenger was measured via spectrophotometry.

The antibacterial activity against Gram-negative E. coli (BNCC133264), AREC and Gram-positive S. aureus (ATCC, 6538), MRSA (∼106 CFU/mL) were evaluated by the spread plate approach. Samples (control group, only PEI@Mn-CDs + H2O2 group, and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs + H2O2 group) were put in a 10 mL glass test tube, and 100 μL bacterial suspensions (∼106 CFU/mL) were placed into samples. After incubating with the material for 20 min at 37.0 °C, 100 μL of the dilution was spread into an agar plate. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the number of bacterial clone was counted for calculating bacterial survival rate according to the following formula: survival ratio (%) = CFUsample × 100 %/CFUcontrol.

Among them, the control group was no treated, the PEI@Mn-CDs + H2O2 group was incubated at 37.0 °C for 20 min with 20.0 μg/mL PEI@Mn-CDs and 100.0 μM H2O2, and the PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs + H2O2 group was incubated at 37.0 °C for 20 min with 20.0 μg/mL PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs and 100.0 μM H2O2.

10 μL of E. coli, AREC, S. aureus, and MRSA suspensions (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) and 990 μL of liquid culture medium were respectively taken and added to 24-well plates, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 12 h. After slowly aspirating the culture medium from each well, the biofilm was rinsed with PBS for 2–3 times to obtain the biofilm. The PBS buffer, H2O2, PEI@Mn-CDs + H2O2 and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs + H2O2 were respectively mixed with the bacterial biofilm and further incubated at 37.0 °C for 48 h until the bacteria formed a mature biofilm. After the floating bacteria and materials were removed by rinsing with methanol, they were fixed with 200 μL of 4 % paraformaldehyde for 2 h and photographed. Finally, the CV staining method was employed to evaluate the clearance effect of nanozymes on the biofilm: Firstly, 200 μL of 0.1 % CV solution was added to each well. After natural air drying, the biofilm was incubated for 20 min. Finally, 100 μL of acetic acid solution (33 %) was added to each well to dissolve the CV, and the absorbance at 595 nm was determined using an enzyme-labeled instrument.

The sterile cell slides with AREC or MRSA biofilms were constructed on 24-well plates by adopting the aforementioned method. Subsequently, the E. coli, AREC, S. aureus, and MRSA biofilms were respectively subjected to four distinct treatments: control (untreated), H2O2, PEI@Mn-CDs + H2O2, and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs + H2O2, and incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. For the observation of live/dead bacterial staining, the biofilms on the slides were stained with SYTO-9 and PI in the dark for 15 min. Subsequently, the slides with adhered biofilms were gently rinsed three times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) to conduct fluorescence imaging using a fluorescence imaging microscope. The excitation wavelength for the red fluorescence image was 590–650 nm, and that for the green fluorescence image was 510–550 nm.

All the biofilms on the sterile cell coverslips were treated with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde fixative solution, and fixed overnight at 4.0 °C for intuitive SEM analysis. After fixation, the mixture was rinsed with ultrapure water. Subsequently, 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, and 100 % ethanol were used to treat the samples for 15 min respectively to gradually dehydrate the bacteria. For scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observation, the final bacterial solution was poured onto a gold-coated silica substrate. The biofilm was stained with FDA, and the penetration and accumulation of the biofilm were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

109 CFU/mL of E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA were placed in sterile glass tubes and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The subsequent treatments were carried out as follows: a. 200 mM H2O2; b. 30 μg/mL PEI@Mn-CDs + 200 mM H2O2; c. 30 μg/mL PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(liquid); d. 30 μg/mL PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) + 200 mM H2O2. Then 10 µM DCFH-DA was added to each well for 30 min at 37 °C to reliably measure levels of ROS in live cells. The cells were washed with PBS for 3 times, and measured for emission at 525 nm with an excitation of 488 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion regulation of PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that metal-based carbon dots with high enzyme-like activity (e.g. POD-like activity, OXD-like activity, GSH-like activity, etc.) can be used as nanozyme for antibacterial and antibiofilm [32–34]. Further study demonstrated that the doped metal ions on carbon dots can be served as catalytic centers to regulate the enzyme-like catalytic reactions [3,35]. The functional groups on the surface of carbon dots including carbonyl, carboxyl group, hydroxyl group and amine group are the catalytic site, and can also provide a binding site for post-modification of metal ions [36,37]. PEI, as a cationic polymer containing abundant primary, secondary, and tertiary amine functions, can provide multiple binding sites for the doping of metal ions [8]. Therefore, PEI was used as the precursor material for carbon dots preparation by chelating with non-toxic metal ion Mn2+, and introducing other oxygen-containing groups to prepare PEI@Mn-CDs that shows a weak enzyme-like activity. The abundant amino groups in PEI can promote its stable coordination with Mn. Multiple non-toxic metal ions were incubated with PEI@Mn-CDs to prepare PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs to further improve the enzyme-like activity, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 2 a, an obvious color change (orange to yellow) of PEI@Mn-CDs solution can be observed after the modification of Cu+/Cu2+. Surprisingly, with the increase of incubation time of PEI@Mn-CDs and Cu+, an obvious precipitation was observed (Inset of Fig. 2a). In contrast, Cu2+ only produced a very small amount of precipitate after incubating with PEI@Mn-CDs for 120 min under the same conditions.

The enzymatic activity of carbon dots-based nanozyme is crucial for generating free radicals, achieving antibacterial effects, and destroying bacterial biofilms [38]. The enzyme-like activities of PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs were also evaluated by using different substrate. Fig. 2b-e shows that the formation of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) after Cu+ modification could result in a significant enhancement of POD-like, GSH-like and CAT-like activities, generate new OXD-like and laccase-like activities. Meanwhile, the modification of Cu2+ and Fe3+ could enhance POD-like activity to a certain extent (Fig. 2b), which might be due to the Fenton co-catalyst system [28]. For the GSH-like activity, the result (Fig. 2f) showed that the modification of Cu2+, Cu+(solution), Fe3+, Co2+, Sn4+, Ce2+, Ca2+ and Cu+(solid) could also bring the varying degrees of improvement. Both Cu+ and Cu2+ modification showed the greatest improvement in GSH-like activity, and the overall order of GSH loss is PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs(51.4 %) > PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid)(37.3 %) > PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(24.4 %) > PEI@Mn-CDs(5.0 %) (Fig. S1). As a result, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs were selected and used in the following experiments.

PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid were prepared according to the metal-amine/carboxyl chelation strategy (Fig. S2). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) revealed that the obtained PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (Fig. 3 a) was flocculent aggregation morphological structure. The crystallinity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs was revealed by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) in the inserted picture of Fig. 3b, and the lattice fringe of 0.301 nm and 293 nm on the surface corresponded to the (002) plane-spacing graphite carbon [39]. The lattice fringes of 0.288 nm and 0.203 nm reveal the presence of manganese oxides and Cu2O, respectively. Notably, the lattice fringe in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) disappeared (Fig. 3c), which may be due to the addition of Cu+ causing the material to aggregate and precipitate, and the surface is covered by PEI. The morphology of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid were also further proved by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra as shown in Fig. 3d. The peaks observed at 745, 1405, 1579 and 3200 cm−1 corresponded to the characteristic vibrations of –C–O, −C–N, –NH and –OH, respectively. For the solution of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, the peaks at about 3200 cm−1 slightly narrower characteristic peak due to hydrogen bonding in the presence of water. However, only the characteristic peaks of –NH and –OH can be observed in the PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid), and the fine characteristic peaks of <1500 cm−1 disappear, which is consistent with TEM results. Meanwhile, X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization revealed their crystal structure (Fig. 3e). Obviously, an obviously broad peak with a center position at approximately 2θ = 23.5° indicated the presence of a large number of functional groups in PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs [40]. Compared with PEI@Mn-CDs, the characteristic peaks appeared at 2θ = 16.2, 28.4 and 43.2° are attributed to CuCl2 (JCPDS 01-081-1841). After precipitation, the broad peak at 23.5° disappears in the spectrum of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid). In addition, an adsorption peak was observed at 460 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of PEI@Mn-CDs (Fig. 3f), which proved the chelation of Mn with the –NH/–NH2. When the excitation wavelength was 468 nm, the emission wavelength was 567 nm with pale green light.

To further determine the complexation doping of Cu+, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3g, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid mainly include C, N, O, Mn and Cu elements, while Cu element is lacking in PEI@Mn-CDs, clearly proving the successfully doping of Cu+. For the Cu 2p spectra in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (Fig. 3h), the characteristic peaks at 952.2 eV and 932.4 eV are originated from Cu+. Meanwhile, two weakly peaks at 954.6 eV and 934.7 eV are observed, which correspond to the Cu2+. These results indicated that the added Cu+ was partially oxidized to Cu2+ by PEI@Mn-CDs. The presence of Cu in different valence states (i.e. Cu2+/Cu+) is conducive to promoting electron transfer and enhancing enzyme catalytic activity. Furthermore, it can be seen from Fig. 3h that the ratio of Cu+/Cu2+ in the solid is significantly higher than that in the liquid. According to previous research [28], Cu+/Cu2+ acts as the active site, and an increase in its ratio is conducive to enhancing the peroxidase-like activity of the nanozyme. The Mn 2p spectra (Fig. 3i) in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs show distinct peaks at 653.1 eV and 642.6 eV, referring to Mn3+. The peaks at 650.5 eV and 641.0 eV indicating the presence of Mn2+. Compared with the results of PEI@Mn-CDs, the ratio of Mn3+/Mn2+ significantly increased after the modification of Cu. According to the results of XPS, the content proportion of the Mn element in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (solid) (2.5 %) also significantly decreased compared with that in the liquid PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (4.05 %) and PEI@Mn-CDs (5.47 %), revealing in the addition of Cu occupies some of the coordination sites of Mn. The C 1 s region consisted of three peaks at 288.3.8, 286.1 and 284.6 eV, in accordance with the C=O, C–O and C–C groups, respectively (Fig. S3a). The two peaks in N 1 s at 400.5 eV and 399.2 eV are attributed to pyrrolic N and aromatic N (Fig. S3b). The XPS spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. S3c) exhibits the presence of O-metal (i.e. O-Cu/Mn). These results demonstrated the successful complexation doping of Cu+.

The oxidation of the chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 was employed as a typical catalytic reaction to investigate the POD-like activity of PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid. As showed in Fig. 4 a, the absorption peak at 652 nm belongs to the oxidation product ox-TMB (blue color). Compared with PEI@Mn-CDs, the addition of Cu+ and Cu2+ both promoted the oxidation of TMB, and the catalytic performance followed the order Cu+(solid) > Cu2+ > Cu+. In particular, the addition of Cu+ leads to the formation of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs precipitation, which can significantly increase the absorbance of the system from 0.502 for PEI@Mn-CDs to 2.975 for PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid). 4-AP + 2,4-DP was used as a substrate to evaluate the laccase-like activity of the prepared nanozymes, which produces a red product and has an absorption peak at 510 nm. As depicted in Fig. 4b, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs shows the laccase-like activity, of which PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) shows the highest activity. The PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs could not promote the red product generation, and showed an obvious peak at 418 nm, which might be that PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs interact separately with 4-AP or 2,4-DP. In the absorbance of H2O2, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) could significantly catalyze the oxidation of TMB into blue ox-TMB, indicating that it also has OXD-like activity (Fig. 4c). Meanwhile, the CAT-like activity was studied by the reaction of H2O2 with Ti4+ tetrad to produce H2O2-Ti4+ complex with characteristic absorption peak at 376 nm. The absorbance at 376 nm will gradually decrease with the increase of the CAT-like activity of the nanozyme. As shown in Fig. 4d, PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid both exhibit lower absorbance than “H2O2 + Ti4+” and “H2O2 + PEI@Mn-CDs + Ti4+” groups, indicating the well CAT-like activity, and the order of catalytic activity is PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) > PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs > PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs > PEI@Mn-CDs. Importantly, the results in Fig. 2f also demonstrates that the loss rate of GSH also increased with the modification of Cu, and the GSH depleting contributed to the improvement of antibacterial effect [34]. These results revealed that the addition of Cu+, especially the formation of precipitation, could significantly improve the POD-like, CAT-like and GSH-like activities, and could promote PEI@Mn-CDs to generate OXD-like and laccase-like activities, which is conducive to improving antibacterial activity. Particularly, the POD-like activity of nanozyme catalyzes the
OH produced by H2O2, which has a good effect in antibacterial.

In general, the POD-like activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs is pH-dependent with an optimum activity at about pH 4 (Fig. 4e), which is consistent with the result that the activity of HRP is optimal at pH = 4 [41]. As shown in the inset picture of Fig. 4e, when pH is greater than 6, the POD-like activity disappears. Interestingly, the optimal pH of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was also investigated through performing colorimetric analysis. It was found (Fig. 4f) that the optimal pH changes from 4 of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs to 6–7 of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid). PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) maintains POD-like activity over a wide range of pH 3–8, which is of great help for practical antibacterial applications. Afterward, the Michaelis-Menten equation was applied for the evaluation of the POD-like activity of the prepared nanozymes by exploiting TMB and H2O2 as the substrates (Fig. S4). The kinetic constants V max and K m of PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid were listed in Table 1 . We compared the POD-like activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) and other prepared nanozymes. When TMB was used as the reaction substrate, the K m of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was lower than the other nanozymes, indicating the stronger affinity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) for its substrate according to the Michaelis-Menten model [34]. The V max of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was ∼4.9-fold higher than the solution of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, and ∼10.48-fold higher than the PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, and ∼29.7-fold higher than PEI@Mn-CDs. Meanwhile, when H2O2 was used as the reaction substrate, the V max of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was significantly superior over the other nanozymes. Based on above, the wide pH application range of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) and high catalytic activity are mainly due to the accumulation and sedimentation caused by Cu+ addition and the protection of the active site of the nanozyme by PEI coating, which are consistent with the results of characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial efficacy of nanozyme in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Taking in to account the different metal ions with different ability to regulate enzyme activity, plate counting method was applied to detect the inhibition ability of PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs on E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA. In the presence of H2O2, the results proved (Fig. 5 a–d) that compared with the control group, the addition of Fe3+, Zn2+, Ce2+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ could produce weak antibacterial effects on one or two kinds of bacteria to a certain extent, but could not achieve resistance to four kinds of bacteria at the same time. Importantly, the addition of Cu+ and Cu2+ could achieve simultaneous resistance to E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA. As can been seen from Fig. 5e, the antibacterial efficiencies of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs against S. aureus and MRSA can reach over 91.7 %. The antibacterial efficiencies of PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs against E. coli and AREC are 74.9 % and 41.9 % respectively. After substituting Cu2+ with Cu+, the antibacterial efficiencies increase to 92.8 % and 75.8 %, respectively, because the introduction of Cu+ can significantly enhance the activity of nanozyme (Fig. 4). Meanwhile, after the formation of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs precipitation, the antibacterial effect against the four bacteria (i.e. E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA) was further significantly enhanced, with antibacterial efficiencies reaching 100 %, which is mainly due to the fact that the formation of precipitates leads to the enhancement of multi-enzyme catalytic activity while producing new OXD-like and laccase −like activities. We further investigated the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of PEI@Mn-CDs, the PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid. According to Figs. S5–S8, the MIC of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was 4 µg/mL, which is significantly superior over PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs. Additionally, we compared the MIC of PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs with other reported nanozymes. As shown in Table S1, the MIC of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) is still advantageous in the majority of the reported nanozymes. It is worth noting that PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) was designed by post-modification for Cu doping, which is different from those reported carbon-based antibacterial materials in the literature [42,43].

To explore the
OH generation, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) as a fluorescence indicator was applied to compare the catalytic activities of PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid. The results (Fig. 5f-g) revealed that PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) could catalyze H2O2 for producing
OH in bacterial. At the same conditions, the fluorescence intensity of DCF (transformed by DCFH-DA) in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (solid) group was much higher than that in PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs groups (Fig. 5f). Meanwhile, the live/dead bacteria staining experiments was used for the visual observation of these bacteria by two kinds of fluorescent nucleic acid dyes SYTO-9/PI [44]. It is well known that SYTO-9 can enter all bacterial cells and bind to DNA, emitting green fluorescence, while PI can only penetrate dead bacterial cells and emit red fluorescence. When both dyes are present in dead bacterial cells, the fluorescence of SYTO-9 is significantly reduced. As shown in Fig. 5h, after treatment with PEI@Mn-CDs, the bacteria exhibited weak red and bright green fluorescence. In contrast, upon treatment with PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, the red fluorescence of the bacteria was enhanced, while the green fluorescence was attenuated. In the PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) group, almost no green fluorescence signal was detected. These facts implied that PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) could effectively induce H2O2 to generate
OH into bacterial, resulting in the dead bacteria.

The morphology of the bacteria after incubation with PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs was further analyzed, and the results are shown in Fig. 5i. It can be seen that the bacteria presented a complete morphology, but after treatment with PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, the morphology of the bacteria collapsed, and even completely deformed, and the surface bacteria were completely killed. Considering that the prepared PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs, serving as carbon dots, would emit yellow fluorescence, fluorescence inverted microscope was applied to investigate the permeation of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs. As presented in Fig. 5j, compared with the control and the bright field, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs is capable of entering the interiors of E. coli, AREC, S. aureus, and MRSA. Thus, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs can achieve highly efficient antibacterial activity.

Similar to other carbon dots, the prepared PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs also possessed photo-luminescent properties. In Fig. 6 a, PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs emitted light green with ultraviolet light excitation (365 nm), and the fluorescence of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs almost disappeared due to precipitation. Interestingly, it was found that the color of PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid could converted to yellow or yellow-green (inset of Fig. 6a) after the addition of H2O2. Meanwhile, compared with the absence of H2O2, the solid of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs would gradually dissolve in the presence of H2O2 and emit yellow-green fluorescence (Fig. S9), which is beneficial for the POD-like activity. As shown in Fig. 3f, Figs. S10 and S11, with the addition of H2O2, the maximum emission wavelengths of both PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs show a certain degree of redshift. The color of PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs(solid) is pale yellowish green (Fig. S12). Meanwhile, we further investigated the UV–vis absorbance spectra of PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Xn+-CDs in the presence/absence of H2O2. As observed in Fig. 6b, PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs show an obviously peak at 416 nm. The addition of H2O2 has no significant effect on the characteristic peak of PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, but the characteristic peak of PEI@Mn-CDs is redshifted from 416 nm to 448 nm in the presence of H2O2. Notably, the absorption peak of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs also appears at 448 nm, which is consistent with that of PEI@Mn-CDs + H2O2 group, and the peak at 448 nm shows a marked increase after the addition of H2O2. This phenomenon is mainly due to the fact that the H2O2 addition and Cu+ modification can react with aniline group in PEI@Mn-CDs and promote the change of the maximum UV absorption, while the addition of Cu2+ can prevent the reaction (caused by H2O2) through occupying the active sites such as aniline and –NH2 groups. Importantly, the results of XPS revealed that the added Cu+ can react with PEI@Mn-CDs to form Cu2+ and be complexed with it (Fig. S13), resulting in the redshift of characteristic peaks. Additionally, we further investigated the influence of dissolved O2 in the PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs + H2O2 and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs + H2O2 groups. As shown in Fig. S14, in the presence of air, the maximum UV absorption at 416 and 448 nm increases significantly, whereas in the presence of N2, the maximum UV absorption at 416 and 448 nm decreases significantly, which is mainly due to the laccase-like activity of the nanozyme.

PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) showed multi-enzyme activity, of which POD-like activity was the strongest. To verify the
OH generation, methylene blue (MB) dyes as the colorimetric probe were applied to analyze the different groups [45]. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, the peak of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) + MB + H2O2 group at 675 nm is the lowest, indicating the highest
OH generation. The OH generation capability, from strongest to weakest, is: PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) > PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs > PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs > PEI@Mn-CDs, which is inconsistent with the results in Fig. 4a. Meanwhile, three different free radical trapping agents were selected to evaluate free radical production in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) + TMB + H2O2 group. With the addition of β-caroten, tempol and DMSO, the POD-like activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) were inhibited. EPR spectra of
OH (Fig. 6e),
O2 − (Fig. 6f) and 1O2 (Fig. 6g) further indicated that the presence of
OH,
O2 − and 1O2 plays an important role in antibacterial. In addition, the improvement of electron transfer efficiency by Cu+ was also investigated through flavin mononucleotide (FMN) + H2O2 system. The absorption peaks of FMN + H2O2 + PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) group at 450 nm and 370 nm vanished, implying the reduction of FMN (Fig. 6h). Compared with FMN + H2O2 + PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, the introduction of Cu+ and the formation of precipitation can significantly improve the electron transfer efficiency [46]. Additionally, it can be seen from the inset picture of Fig. 6h that the liquid PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs is unstable and prone to aggregation, causing the solution to become turbid. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to evaluate the electron transfer ability of different nanozymes and electrode surfaces. As displayed in Fig. 6i, the redox peak can be observed for PEI@Mn-CDs (black curve). After the addition of Cu+, the peak current of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs increased obviously (red curve), which is due to the excellent electron transfer ability of Cu+ and PEI@Mn-CDs. When Cu+ was added and precipitated, the peak of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) increased significantly (blue curve), Which is mainly because the Cu+ content in precipitation tends to be more saturated than that of liquid PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs (inset of Fig. 6a).

The multivalent Mn2+/Mn3+ coordinated with Cu2+/Cu+ is beneficial to boost the multienzyme-like activities. Based on the results of free radical detection and XPS, the different catalytic activities of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) could be deduced as following:
(1) CAT: H2O2 + Cu+ → Cu2+ + O2 +
O2 −
(2) Laccase: Cu+ + O2 → Cu2+ + 1O2
(3) OXD: Cu+ + 1O2 → Cu2+
(4) POD: Cu+/Mn2+ + H2O2 →
OH + Cu2+/Mn3+
(5) GSH: Cu2+/Mn3+ + GSH → GSSH + Cu+/Mn2+

The POD-like activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) is the most prominent and plays a major role in the antibacterial process. Comprehensively considering the valence state distribution of Cu and Mn, as well as the results of Fig. 6d and Fig. S14, the synergistic catalytic path of Cu and Mn for the formation of free radical during the POD-like activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) can be inferred:

Cu+ + H2O2 → Cu2+ +
OH + −OH(6)

Mn2+ + H2O2 → Mn3+ +
OH + −OH(8)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and antibiofilm activity of nanozymes in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The MTT assay was performed to explore the cytotoxicity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) to evaluate the viability of Hela and HCT 116 cells. As shown in Fig. S15, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) has little effect at high concentration (30 μg/mL) and incubation for 24 h for HCT116 cells, and has no effect at high concentration (70 μg/mL) and incubation for 24 h for Hela. Besides, the MIC concentration of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) is 4 μg/mL, which is much lower than 30 μg/mL for HCT116 cells and 70 μg/mL for Hela cells. These results showed that the PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) exhibits good cytocompatibility and good application potential in antibacterial and other medical/biological research.

Considering the excellent antibacterial activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid), we further explored its antibiofilm capability. Classical crystal violet (CV) staining assay was applied to evaluate the eradication of biofilm using PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid. As can been seen from Fig. 7 a and b, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) could effectively scavenge the biofilm biomass. Through the quantitative analysis of CV staining, low concentration of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) can achieve the removal efficiency of 98.21 % and 98.3 % for AREC and MRSA, respectively. To further evaluate the effects of PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid on biofilm eradication. The fluorescence dyes SYTO-9 was added to the biofilms of E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA after treated by PEI@Mn-CDs, PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid, followed by visual observation of biofilm status by laser confocal microscopy (CLSM). Fig. 7c showed that in PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) group, only a slight green fluorescence signal was present in four different biofilms, indicating a very small number of bacteria survive. Through the quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity in Fig. 7d, over 91.3 % (E. coli), 85.8 % (AREC), 86.0 % (S. aureus) and 88.4 % (MRSA) of biofilm biomass were scavenged by PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid), respectively, while only 27.8 % (E. coli), 55.5 % (AREC), 50.1 % (S. aureus) and 7.8 % (MRSA) of biofilm biomass were reduced by PEI@Mn-CDs in the same conditions. These findings confirmed that PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) can effectively disrupt the biofilms of E. coli, AREC, S. aureus and MRSA.

Generally, carbon dot-based nanozymes will gradually undergo agglomeration, aggregation and precipitation over time, thus severely affecting the stability of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs. The stability of the nanozymes (including PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid) was investigated by evaluating the enzyme-like activity and antibacterial activity after 4 months of storage. As shown in Fig. S16, the stability of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) is significantly stronger than that of its liquid, revealing that the solid form of nanozymes is more conducive to maintaining their stability. Moreover, the presence of Cu+ will also be oxidized, which plays a very important role in the enzyme-catalyzed process [28]. Meanwhile, the antibacterial activity of nanozymes after 4 months of storage was also carefully investigated in comparison with antibiotics (Gentamicin for E. coli and AREC; Cephalexin for S. aureus and MRSA). The results (Fig. S17) indicated that at the same concentration of 20 µg/mL, the solid of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs still exhibited superior antibacterial effects compared to the antibiotics. These results revealed that, compared with the characteristics of liquid carbon dot-based nanozymes that are prone to aggregation and oxidation, the formation of solid state can ensure the long-term stability and antibacterial activity of nanozymes.

In summary, we reported the post-modification of PEI@Mn-CDs nanozyme with metal ions, which enhanced the multi enzyme-like activity and antibacterial properties of the nanozyme. Notably, the modification of Cu+ and the resulting nanozyme precipitation (i.e. PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs and its solid) resulted in a 10.48-fold and 29.7-fold increase in V max compared to PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs and PEI@Mn-CDs, respectively, as well as lower K m value and wider range of pH applications. The results revealed that Cu+ was integrated with PEI@Mn-CDs by the metal-amine/carboxyl coordination strategy and accompanied by Cu2+ generation, which could significantly improve the electron transfer efficiency and free radical generation (including
OH,
O2 − and 1O2). Compared with PEI@Mn-CDs and PEI@Mn/Cu2+-CDs, the super POD-like activity and other enzyme-like activity of PEI@Mn/Cu+-CDs(solid) made it to efficiently antibacterial and eliminate biofilms by entering the interior of bacteria and then generating free radicals. This study presented a superior synergistic strategy to design nanozyme by post-modification of metal ions for efficient antibacterial and antibiofilm. Additionally, further research is needed to investigate the microbial contamination caused by bacterial biofilms in many media, such as wound bacterial infection, water, food, and the environment.

Qiuting Duan: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Linyan Tang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Sanchun Xu: Methodology. Dezhi Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ya Xu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis.
